era_context: The late 15th century, a period of state consolidation in Europe, maritime exploration, and the height of the Italian Renaissance. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty maintained stability, while the Ottoman Empire continued its expansion. In the Americas, the Inca Empire was at its territorial peak. This was a transitional moment between medieval and early modern political structures.

events.title: Skanderbeg (Gjergj Kastrioti) dies
events.description: Skanderbeg, the Albanian military leader who resisted Ottoman expansion for approximately 25 years, died on 17 January 1468 (some sources cite 1467). His death removed the primary military obstacle to Ottoman consolidation in Albania and the southern Balkans. Skanderbeg had maintained Albanian independence through a combination of guerrilla warfare, diplomatic alliances with Venice and the Papacy, and strategic fortifications. His death was a turning point; within a decade, the Ottoman Empire completed its conquest of Albania.

sources.name: Marin Barleti. Vita et Gesta Scanderbegi (written 1508, based on earlier sources)
Ottoman court records and Venetian diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Skanderbeg's death date is well-documented in Ottoman, Venetian, and papal sources. The date 17 January 1468 is widely accepted in modern scholarship.

events.title: Cosimo de' Medici dies; Lorenzo de' Medici assumes leadership of Florence
events.description: Cosimo de' Medici, the patriarch of the Medici family and de facto ruler of Florence, died on 1 August 1464 (some sources cite 1468 for the transition of full power to his grandson Lorenzo). Lorenzo de' Medici, known as 'Lorenzo the Magnificent,' assumed leadership of the Medici family and Florence's political affairs. Lorenzo became one of the most influential patrons of the Italian Renaissance, supporting artists, architects, and humanist scholars. His rule marked the height of Florentine cultural and political influence in Italy.
key_figures: Cosimo de' Medici (deceased, or transitioning power)

sources.name: Medici family correspondence and Florentine state records
Vespasiano da Bisticci. Lives of Illustrious Men

events.certainty_note: Cosimo de' Medici died in 1464, but the transition of full power to Lorenzo occurred gradually. By 1468–1469, Lorenzo had assumed primary leadership. The exact year of transition is somewhat ambiguous in sources.

events.title: Sonni Ali of Songhai defeats Timbuktu and consolidates Songhai Empire
events.region: West Africa, Songhai Empire (modern Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger)
events.description: Sonni Ali (also known as Ali Ber), the ruler of the Songhai Empire, captured Timbuktu from the Tuareg in 1468 and consolidated Songhai's control over the Niger River valley. This conquest marked the beginning of Songhai's rise as the dominant power in West Africa, replacing the declining Mali Empire. Sonni Ali's military campaigns expanded Songhai's territory and wealth, based on control of trans-Saharan trade routes. His reign (1464–1492) transformed Songhai into one of the largest and most powerful African empires of the era.
key_figures: Askia Muhammad (successor, not yet in power)

sources.name: Tarikh al-Sudan (History of Sudan) by al-Sadi, written 17th century
Tarikh al-Fattash by Mahmud Kati, written 16th–17th century
Arab geographers and traders' accounts (Ibn Battuta, al-Idrisi, etc.)

events.certainty_note: Sonni Ali's conquest of Timbuktu is attested in later Arabic chronicles written in the 16th–17th centuries. The date 1468 is conventional; exact dating is uncertain due to the reliance on oral traditions and later compilations. The event is historically significant but the precise year should be treated as approximate (±2–3 years).

events.title: Portuguese explorers reach Cape Verde
events.description: Portuguese explorers, continuing Prince Henry the Navigator's maritime program, reached and began to settle the Cape Verde Islands off the coast of West Africa. The islands were uninhabited and became a Portuguese colony, serving as a way station for ships traveling to and from Africa and later the Americas. The settlement of Cape Verde marked another step in Portugal's systematic exploration and colonization of the Atlantic.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal patron)
António da Noli (explorer, attributed with discovery)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara. Crónica da Guiné
Archaeological evidence from Cape Verde settlements

events.certainty_note: Portuguese discovery and settlement of Cape Verde is traditionally dated to 1460–1462, but sustained settlement and colonization efforts continued through the 1460s–1470s. The date 1468 represents the consolidation of Portuguese control.

events.title: Matthias Corvinus of Hungary defeats Ottoman forces at Vaslui
events.description: King Matthias Corvinus of Hungary led a military campaign against Ottoman expansion in the Balkans, achieving a notable victory at Vaslui (in modern-day Romania) in 1468. This victory temporarily halted Ottoman advances into Hungary and Wallachia, though it did not reverse Ottoman territorial gains. Matthias Corvinus was one of the most capable military leaders of the era and used his victories to strengthen Hungary's position as a regional power.

sources.name: Hungarian royal chronicles and court records

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Vaslui and Matthias Corvinus's military campaigns are well-documented in Hungarian, Ottoman, and Venetian sources. The date 1468 is secure.
cross_references: 1479-001 (Peace treaty between Hungary and Ottoman Empire)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Skanderbeg's resistance is sometimes romanticized in nationalist historiography as a major obstacle to Ottoman expansion. However, modern scholarship suggests that Ottoman resources and military organization were so superior that Skanderbeg's resistance, while militarily impressive, had limited strategic impact on the broader Ottoman conquest of the Balkans. The Ottoman Empire would have consolidated the region regardless of Skanderbeg's efforts, though perhaps with a longer timeline. Additionally, the date of Cosimo de' Medici's death (1464) and the transition of power to Lorenzo is sometimes conflated; Lorenzo did not assume full leadership until after his father Piero's death in 1469.
historiographic_note: European events in 1468 are well-documented through archival sources, diplomatic correspondence, and near-contemporary chronicles. African history relies heavily on later Arabic chronicles (16th–17th centuries) that recorded oral traditions, introducing significant dating uncertainty. Ottoman sources are reliable for military and administrative matters but less detailed on cultural and economic history. The historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; the lives of ordinary people, women, and non-elite actors are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Skanderbeg's death removed the primary obstacle to Ottoman consolidation in Albania.
Sonni Ali's consolidation of Songhai power led to the empire's dominance in West Africa until his death in 1492.
Matthias Corvinus's military victories against the Ottomans eventually led to a peace treaty in 1479.

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
